articles.title: Ollama's Chinese Model Support Is Real — But Running Kimi and DeepSeek Locally Has a Hidden Cost
articles.excerpt: Your error rate just spiked 12%. Three weeks of debugging, $40k in developer hours, and the coffee's cold. The terminal is still red. You've been burning through API credits calling a US-based LLM, an…
articles.body: Your error rate just spiked 12%. Three weeks of debugging, $40k in developer hours, and the coffee's cold. The terminal is still red. You've been burning through API credits calling a US-based LLM, and every query that touches proprietary code feels like handing your competitor a roadmap. Now imagine you could run that same model locally. On your own GPU. Zero data leaving your infrastructure. That's the promise behind Ollama's recent expansion to support Chinese AI models — Kimi-K2.5, GLM-5, MiniMax, and DeepSeek. And the V2EX discussion around this is revealing something the Western dev community hasn't fully grasped yet: these models aren't just cheaper alternatives. They're a different paradigm for AI infrastructure — one that comes with trade-offs nobody's talking about.
articles.author: xu xu

articles.title: Adding one field to our CAPI payload moved match rate from 66% to 79% overnight
articles.excerpt: Adding hashed phone number to an already-live CAPI integration — nothing else — pushed match rate 13 points in a single day. That's the kind of result that makes you realize the integration being "on"…
articles.body: Adding hashed phone number to an already-live CAPI integration — nothing else — pushed match rate 13 points in a single day. That's the kind of result that makes you realize the integration being "on" and the integration actually working are two different things. Most accounts I audit have CAPI running. Most of them are only passing hashed email ( em ). That feels complete because email is the obvious identity field, but Meta's matching logic doesn't weight all parameters equally across all users. In Korean markets especially, a large share of accounts are tied to Kakao-linked emails — addresses users created for one service and never check. The phone number is the stronger identity anchor. When I added ph from checkout form data on one D2C account, match rate jumped from 66% to 79%.
articles.author: 강해수

articles.title: Watch live: Rocket Lab launching 'Ten Owl of Ten' mission for Synspective today
articles.excerpt: Rocket Lab is poised to launch the "Ten Owl of Ten" mission from New Zealand today (June 26), adding to Japanese company Synspective's imaging constellation. Watch it live here.
articles.body: Rocket Lab is scheduled to launch another Strix satellite for Japanese company Synspective's imaging constellation, in orbit above the island nation. An Electron rocket will lift off from Rocket Lab's Launch Complex-1 (LC-1) in New Zealand today (June 26), during a window that opens at 12:45 p.m. EDT (1645 GMT; 4:45 a.m. on June 27 local New Zealand time). As its name suggests, the "Ten Owl of Ten" mission will launch Synspective's 10th synthetic aperture radar (SAR) satellite, which will expand the company's network above Japan to provide imaging data for use in city planning, monitoring infrastructure and responding to natural disasters. You can watch it here at Space.com, courtesy of Rocket Lab, as well as on the company's website .
articles.author: Josh Dinner

articles.title: Fan-Out/Fan-In and the Async HTTP Pattern
articles.excerpt: You have 500 order line items to process and no ordering dependency between them, so the question is why the workflow takes as long as 500 activities run back to back when nothing forces them to. The…
articles.body: You have 500 order line items to process and no ordering dependency between them, so the question is why the workflow takes as long as 500 activities run back to back when nothing forces them to. The chaining pattern from Part 1 awaits each activity before scheduling the next, which is exactly right when step two needs step one's output and exactly wasteful when the items are independent. The fix is fan-out/fan-in: schedule all the activities at once, then aggregate the results, and the only real trick is doing it in a way that survives the orchestrator replaying. There is also a one-character version of this code that compiles, runs, and silently throws the parallelism away, which is the bug this article spends the most time on.
articles.author: Martin Oehlert

articles.title: When Your Coding Agent Needs a Scribe, Not a Memory Engine
articles.excerpt: Over the past few weeks, I have had several conversations about the right way to give AI coding agents persistent memory. Some developers ask about AgentMemory. Others ask about Qiju, which I built an…
articles.body: Over the past few weeks, I have had several conversations about the right way to give AI coding agents persistent memory. Some developers ask about AgentMemory. Others ask about Qiju, which I built and maintain. My usual response is: they are not solving the same problem. But I have not written that distinction down clearly. This post is my attempt to do that. The same surface problem Both tools address the same frustration. Every new coding session starts without context. The agent does not know which file is authoritative. It does not know what was decided in the previous session. It does not know which approaches have already failed or which assumptions have been verified. The developer spends the opening minutes of every session reconstructing what the previous session established.
articles.author: Jason Shen

articles.title: The Day My Research Assistant Finally Got a Memory
articles.excerpt: I've spent the last few weeks wrestling with a problem that I suspect many AI builders share: my research assistant agent was smart, but it had the memory of a goldfish and the spending habits of a tr…
articles.body: I've spent the last few weeks wrestling with a problem that I suspect many AI builders share: my research assistant agent was smart, but it had the memory of a goldfish and the spending habits of a trust fund kid. Every time I asked it to help me find research papers on AI/ML topics, it would recommend articles I'd already read. It would suggest the same paper three times in a single conversation. And worse—it was using GPT-4 for every single query, even when a simpler model would have worked fine. So I built something better. A research assistant that actually remembers what I've read and thinks about how much each query costs before it runs. Here's how I did it. The Problem: Amnesia Is Expensive Let me show you what I mean.
articles.author: Sasidhar Prathipati

articles.title: # Treat: A Global Gifting Platform Empowering Local Businesses
articles.excerpt: This project was built for the purposes of entering the H0 Hackathon. 🚀 Live at: https://www.sendatreat.app/ Gifting is one of the most natural human expressions of connection. People love surprising…
articles.body: This project was built for the purposes of entering the H0 Hackathon. 🚀 Live at: https://www.sendatreat.app/ The Problem Gifting is one of the most natural human expressions of connection. People love surprising friends, family, and colleagues with food, gifts, services, and experiences. However, the current experience is fragmented: People rely on multiple apps and coordination tools Most gifting defaults to cash transfers, losing emotional intent Real-world fulfillment is disconnected from digital intent At the same time, millions of local businesses globally — from restaurants and food vendors to salons, hotels, and service providers — struggle to reach customers consistently without expensive marketing platforms or technical infrastructure.
articles.author: Seth David Gyimah

articles.title: Stop Impossible States: State Machines in React ⚡
articles.excerpt: The "Boolean Soup" Disaster When building complex, multi-step interfaces at Smart Tech Devs—like an enterprise payment wizard or a data integration pipeline—developers naturally reach for React's useS…
articles.body: The "Boolean Soup" Disaster When building complex, multi-step interfaces at Smart Tech Devs—like an enterprise payment wizard or a data integration pipeline—developers naturally reach for React's useState . You define isLoading , isError , isSuccess , and isIdle . This creates a massive architectural flaw known as Boolean Soup . If you have four boolean variables, your component mathematically has 16 possible states (2^4). But in reality, a form can only be in one state at a time. Due to asynchronous race conditions or unhandled click events, it is incredibly easy to accidentally set both isLoading: true AND isError: true simultaneously. The UI glitches out, rendering a loading spinner overlapping a red error banner.
articles.author: Prajapati Paresh

articles.body: TL;DR Key specs of the Nothing Phone 4b have leaked courtesy of tipster Yogesh Brar. The phone is expected to come in three colorways and two storage configurations. Just a day after Nothing officially revealed the design of the Nothing Phone 4b , tipster Yogesh Brar has now leaked the phone’s key specs and color options. articles.title: The LEGO Polaroid OneStep SX-70 Camera Is on Sale for the Last Day of Prime Day
articles.excerpt: The LEGO Polaroid OneStep SX-70 Camera Is on Sale for the Last Day of Prime Day
articles.body: The LEGO Polaroid OneStep SX-70 Camera is currently on sale for 18% off. But act fast; that's only until the end of Prime Day, which ends tomorrow, Friday, June 26. The camera is a LEGO Ideas set , which means that it originated as a fan proposal that was later voted on, by the LEGO community, to be designed and built into an official set . LEGO Ideas Polaroid OneStep SX-70 First introduced in 1972, the actual Polaroid OneStep SX-70 Camera made instant photography a mainstream phenomenon. The LEGO model is 516 pieces, perfect for an afternoon of intensive building. It includes three 'photos' (no shaking required!) that you load into the camera. When you push the shutter button, a mechanism will eject the photo out.

articles.title: Turn your room into a galaxy: The best Prime Day deals on star projectors for aspiring astronomers
articles.excerpt: Whether light pollution is foiling your stargazing or you just want to transform a room, there are great star projector deals this Prime Day and I've picked out the best.
articles.body: How many star projectors do you need? I still don't have an answer. It started with a blue, Northern-Lights style projector, then one capable of projecting actual stars, albeit not in the right color. Now I'm eyeing up these Prime Day star projector deals, finger over the 'Buy Now' button, and I'm inviting you to join me. Not all star projectors are the same, mind you; some are there purely to create a relaxing display, aiding meditation or lulling a child to sleep. Meanwhile, others are more scientifically accurate, designed to educate rather than relax. It's a sad fact that, thanks to light pollution, the displays these projectors put out can be prettier and clearer than the ones you'll see in your own back garden.

articles.title: Lizzy Caplan Joins FX's Far Cry TV Series
articles.excerpt: Mean Girls and Cloverfield star Lizzy Caplan has been cast in FX's upcoming adaptation.
articles.body: FX's upcoming adaptation of Far Cry has cast Lizzy Caplan, who is best known for Freaks and Geeks, Mean Girls, and Cloverfield. Last year, FX and Ubisoft confirmed that Noah Hawley (showrunner of Fargo and Alien: Earth) will helm an adaptation of Far Cry. The show will be an anthology series with each season telling a new story with a new cast, not unlike the games. As of right now, details are scarce on what the first season will look like, but it's hard to imagine a Far Cry story without a unique location and a psychotic villain. Caplan's role is being kept under wraps, but she will star alongside It's Always Sunny in Philadelphia's Rob Mac (formerly known as Rob McElhenney). Mac's role is also a mystery, but he will also executive-produce the upcoming TV series.
articles.author: Cade Onder

articles.title: Ratatouille 2 Will Never Happen, Says Director Brad Bird
articles.excerpt: Don't expect Pixar's favorite rat to make a return, as director Brad Bird sees no potential in a Ratatouille 2.
articles.body: Don't expect Pixar's favorite rat to make a return, as director Brad Bird sees no potential in a Ratatouille 2 . Speaking with Collider , Bird was asked if he thinks there will be a second Ratatouille movie. Bird made it pretty clear that he believes Remy's story is completed and there's nothing left to do with that world. He noted Pixar has tried to nudge him into making a sequel, but he remains firm in his decision to leave it alone. "No, I don't," said Bird. "They've made little feints towards that to see how I would react.
articles.author: Cade Onder

articles.title: Apple adds Google Gemini coding assistant in Xcode 26.6 update
articles.excerpt: Apple has released Xcode 26.6, adding support for a third coding assistant to the company’s integrated development environment software.
articles.body:
Apple has rolled out an update to its integrated development environment (IDE) software, Xcode. This new version, Xcode 26.6, brings a notable addition for developers working within the company’s ecosystem. It now includes support for Google Gemini, a powerful coding assistant.

The inclusion of Google Gemini marks a significant expansion of Xcode's capabilities. Gemini is known for its advanced AI functions designed to aid in software development. Its integration could streamline various coding tasks and potentially speed up the development process for many.

This update is particularly interesting as Gemini represents the third coding assistant now supported within Xcode. While the specifics of the other two assistants aren't detailed, the growing number of such tools suggests Apple's commitment to

articles.title: Anthropic Thinks Its Own Success Is Key to Making AI Safe
articles.excerpt: Anthropic's critics argue it's rapidly accumulating power. The company says that's what responsible AI development looks like.
articles.body:
Anthropic articulates a strategy where its organizational success is presented as fundamental to ensuring AI safety. The company contends that its operational growth and development trajectory are directly linked to establishing secure and responsible artificial intelligence systems. This perspective positions its expansion as an essential component of its safety mandate.

This approach, however, has drawn considerable attention from critics. These observers argue that Anthropic is rapidly accumulating significant power within the evolving AI landscape. Their concerns focus on the potential implications of such concentrated influence on the broader field of AI development and its future direction.

In response to these critiques, Anthropic asserts that its current scale and influence are precisely what responsible AI development necessitates. The company maintains that its methods and growth are not merely advantageous but are, in fact, crucial for effectively mitigating risks associated with advanced AI. This stance frames its expansion as a deliberate and responsible strategy.

The situation highlights a key divergence in how Anthropic's actions are interpreted. While some view its increasing power with apprehension, Anthropic itself frames this growth as a prerequisite for achieving

articles.title: The White House is asking OpenAI to slow roll the release of its new model over safety concerns
articles.excerpt: penAI reportedly plans to share its newest model, GPT 5.6, with a select group of partners instead of to the broader public. The reason: the Trump administration told it to.
articles.body:
OpenAI is reportedly adjusting its release strategy for its latest model, GPT 5.6. The company intends to share this new iteration with a select group of partners rather than making it publicly available. This measured approach follows a direct request from the Trump administration.

The White House's intervention stems from expressed safety concerns regarding the model's broader release. This suggests a proactive stance from the administration on the potential implications of advanced AI technologies. The specific nature of these safety concerns has not been detailed in the available reporting.

OpenAI's decision to "slow roll" the release to partners indicates a controlled deployment strategy. This approach allows for limited testing and feedback within a more contained environment before any potential wider distribution. For readers seeking more comprehensive details on this situation, the full story is available via TechCrunch.

articles.title: OpenAI will delay GPT-5.6 after Trump administration request
articles.excerpt: The Trump administration, apprehensive of potential security issues, has reportedly asked OpenAI to stagger the release of its next big-ticket model, GPT-5.6. The Information reported that OpenAI CEO…
articles.body: The Trump administration, apprehensive of potential security issues, has reportedly asked OpenAI to stagger the release of its next big-ticket model, GPT-5.6. The Information reported that OpenAI CEO Sam Altman told employees Wednesday in a company Q&A that it would release GPT-5.6 in limited preview form - granting access only to a small group of enterprise customers - in compliance with a request from the federal government. During that preview period, the Trump administration itself would reportedly approve access for customers on a case-by-case basis. It's a more favorable deal than the Trump administration gave OpenAI rival Anthropic … Read the full story at The Verge.

articles.title: Expedition 73 Crew Reflects on Science, Teamwork, and Life in Orbit
articles.excerpt: On June 16, astronauts and cosmonauts gathered at Space Center Houston to share stories from their missions aboard the International Space Station and recognize the teamwork and people on the ground t…
articles.body: The Expedition 73 crew attends a debrief and awards ceremony at Space Center Houston’s IMAX theater on June 16, 2026. NASA/Luna Posadas Nava On June 16, astronauts and cosmonauts gathered at Space Center Houston to share stories from their missions aboard the International Space Station and recognize the teamwork and people on the ground that made their missions possible.  The Expedition 73 Welcome Home Ceremony brought together members of NASA’s SpaceX Crew-10, Soyuz MS-27, and NASA’s SpaceX Crew-11 missions.
